evaluation_inputs.system_description: MINPHIS is a service-oriented health information system designed specifically for the Nigerian and African healthcare context. Originally based on MUMPS language, FileMan-database, and Kernel-package, the system has undergone modernization to a Client-Server architecture utilizing an RPC Broker. It serves as a low-cost, sustainable application for managing patient records, accounting, and medical records across federal, state, and local government healthcare tiers.

phase_1_discovery.initial_documentation_status: Pre-exercise review of architecture documentation was conducted, though architectural approaches required further identification and elicitation during the session.
evaluator_inquiry_questions: How does the system function and behave during execution?
Questions related to run-time behavior (sent via text/email to remote users)

hardware_view_description: Database Layer utilizing FileMan and M Software
RPC Broker Server Layer for middleware communication
runtime_constraints: Web-based system performance with web services

questions: How does the architecture respond to peak load scenarios?
How does the database structure affect performance and migration needs?

raw_candidate_scenarios.description: No unauthorized access to the system (User)
User errors in using system should be handled (User)
All operations are processed in fastest possible speed (User)
Proper data encapsulation and secure data structures (Architect)
Flexible implementation using other programming languages (Architect)
Overall consistent behavior expected from the architecture (Architect)
Framework should be complete, clear and perform exactly as required (Application Developer)

business_goals: Use for Answering ad hoc queries from medical Researchers
Use to Provide performance information (e.g., mortality rates, patient counts)
Produce on-demand & Periodic reports based on specific needs of Institution or Hospital
technical_constraints: An improved web-based system with web services

architectural_approaches.description: The system is structured with a clear separation between client applications and server-side processing, with the RPC Broker facilitating communication between tiers.

addressed_attributes.rationale: Enables distributed processing and reduces client-side computational load by centralizing business logic on the server
Centralized server management allows for better monitoring and maintenance of critical services
Server-side transaction management ensures data consistency and integrity

architectural_approaches.description: The system is organized into distinct layers: Database Layer (FileMan and M Software), RPC Broker Server Layer (middleware), and Client Layer, with clear separation of concerns.

addressed_attributes.rationale: Layer separation allows changes to one layer without affecting others, facilitating the modernization from MUMPS to web-based services
Abstraction between layers enables migration to different platforms while maintaining core functionality
Clear layer boundaries enforce consistent design principles across the system

architectural_approaches.description: Remote Procedure Call (RPC) Broker serves as middleware to handle communication between client applications and the database layer, abstracting the complexity of remote invocations.

addressed_attributes.rationale: Optimizes network communication and reduces overhead through efficient marshalling and unmarshalling of requests
Centralizes authentication and authorization logic, providing a single point of control for access management
Provides a uniform interface for client-server communication, ensuring consistent interaction patterns

architectural_approaches.description: The modernization strategy incorporates web services to expose system functionality, enabling integration with external systems and supporting web-based client access.

addressed_attributes.rationale: Web services expand system capabilities by enabling integration with other healthcare systems and third-party applications
Service interfaces allow for incremental updates and feature additions without disrupting existing functionality
Web services can implement standard security protocols (SSL/TLS, authentication tokens) for secure data exchange
Standard web service protocols enable platform-independent access to system functionality

children.refinement: Transaction response time under peak load
children.scenario_description: Under peak load conditions (150 normalized transactions/second), the system completes transactions within 1 second for 95% of requests

children.refinement: Transaction response time under double peak load
children.scenario_description: Under double peak load conditions (300 normalized transactions/second), the system completes transactions within 4 seconds for 95% of requests
Database storage operations complete within 200ms latency limit during normal operations
Ad hoc query reports are generated within 10ms for standard patient record queries
The RPC Broker and web services layer prevent all unauthorized access attempts to patient records through centralized authentication and authorization
All client-server communications are encrypted using SSL/TLS protocols to protect patient data in transit
All access to patient records is logged with user identity, timestamp, and action performed for compliance auditing
The system maintains 99.5% availability during business hours (8am-6pm) for patient record access
When the RPC Broker server experiences failure, the system provides read-only access to cached patient records within 30 seconds
Server-side transaction management ensures 100% data consistency for patient record updates with automatic rollback on failure
In case of system failure, patient data can be recovered with maximum 15 minutes of data loss (RPO = 15 minutes)
Changes to the database layer (FileMan/M Software) can be implemented without requiring client application updates, completed within 2 weeks
New web service endpoints can be added to expose additional functionality without disrupting existing RPC Broker clients, completed within 1 week

children.refinement: Migration from MUMPS to modern platform
children.scenario_description: The layered architecture supports incremental migration from MUMPS to web-based services while maintaining operational continuity
Web services enable client applications on Windows, Linux, and mobile platforms to access patient records using standard protocols
The RPC Broker abstraction allows migration to alternative database systems without requiring client-side changes
Web services enable integration with third-party healthcare systems (lab systems, pharmacy systems) for data exchange
Healthcare professionals can execute complex ad hoc queries on patient records with results returned within 10ms for standard queries
The RPC Broker provides a consistent interface pattern for all client-server interactions, reducing developer learning curve to 2 weeks
The layered architecture enforces separation of concerns with no direct database access from clients, maintaining design consistency across all modules

scenario_reference.text: Under peak load conditions (150 normalized transactions/second), the system completes transactions within 1 second for 95% of requests

analysis_records.stimulus: 150 normalized transactions per second from multiple clients
analysis_records.response_measure: Transaction completion within 1 second for 95% of requests
architectural_decisions: Client-Server architecture with centralized business logic on server
RPC Broker middleware for efficient request marshalling
Server-side transaction management
analysis_records.reasoning: The RPC Broker optimizes network communication overhead, and centralized server processing reduces client-side load. However, the single-server bottleneck and network latency between client-broker-database layers may impact response times under peak load.

risks.description: RPC Broker server may become a bottleneck under peak load, causing response times to exceed 1 second
risks.rationale: Single RPC Broker instance handling 150 transactions/second without documented load balancing or horizontal scaling mechanisms

risks.description: Network latency between client-broker-database layers may accumulate and exceed 1 second threshold
risks.rationale: Multi-tier architecture introduces multiple network hops; cumulative latency not quantified in current documentation

non_risks.description: Server-side transaction management will not introduce significant overhead for simple transactions
non_risks.rationale: FileMan database is optimized for healthcare transactions; transaction overhead typically <50ms for standard operations

sensitivity_points.parameter: Network bandwidth between client and RPC Broker

conflicting_attributes: Performance (negative: higher isolation increases lock contention and latency)

scenario_reference.text: Under double peak load conditions (300 normalized transactions/second), the system completes transactions within 4 seconds for 95% of requests

analysis_records.stimulus: 300 normalized transactions per second from multiple clients
analysis_records.response_measure: Transaction completion within 4 seconds for 95% of requests
architectural_decisions: Client-Server architecture with centralized processing
RPC Broker as single middleware layer
No documented horizontal scaling or load balancing
analysis_records.reasoning: Doubling the load to 300 transactions/second without architectural provisions for horizontal scaling or load distribution creates significant risk. The 4-second tolerance provides buffer, but single-server architecture lacks elasticity.

risks.description: RPC Broker server will likely fail to meet 4-second response time under 300 transactions/second without horizontal scaling
risks.rationale: No documented load balancing or clustering; single server must handle 2x peak load with only 4x time allowance

risks.description: Database layer (FileMan/M Software) may experience lock contention and degraded performance under doubled load
risks.rationale: MUMPS-based systems have limited concurrency mechanisms; 300 concurrent transactions may exceed database capacity

risks.description: Memory exhaustion on RPC Broker server under sustained double peak load
risks.rationale: No documented memory management or request queuing strategy for overload conditions

tradeoff_points.parameter: Request queuing depth at RPC Broker

scenario_reference.text: The RPC Broker and web services layer prevent all unauthorized access attempts to patient records through centralized authentication and authorization

analysis_records.stimulus: Unauthorized access attempt to patient records
architectural_decisions: Centralized authentication and authorization in RPC Broker
Web services layer with standard security protocols
No direct database access from clients
analysis_records.reasoning: The RPC Broker pattern centralizes security enforcement, creating a single point of control. Layered architecture prevents direct database access. However, the dual access paths (RPC Broker and Web Services) require consistent security policy enforcement.

risks.description: Inconsistent security policies between RPC Broker and Web Services layers may create authorization bypass vulnerabilities
risks.rationale: Two separate access paths require synchronized security configurations; policy drift could allow unauthorized access through one path

risks.description: Session management vulnerabilities if RPC Broker does not implement secure token expiration and renewal
risks.rationale: Long-lived sessions or improper token handling could allow session hijacking attacks

non_risks.description: Layered architecture effectively prevents direct database access bypass
non_risks.rationale: Clear layer boundaries enforce that all access must pass through RPC Broker or Web Services, eliminating direct database connection vulnerabilities

sensitivity_points.parameter: Authorization policy synchronization mechanism between RPC Broker and Web Services

conflicting_attributes: Security (positive: frequent checks reduce unauthorized access window)
Performance (negative: frequent authentication checks add latency)

scenario_reference.text: The system maintains 99.5% availability during business hours (8am-6pm) for patient record access

analysis_records.stimulus: Normal operational conditions with potential component failures
architectural_decisions: Client-Server architecture with centralized RPC Broker
Server-side transaction management
No documented redundancy or failover mechanisms
analysis_records.reasoning: 99.5% availability allows only 3 minutes of downtime per business day. The centralized RPC Broker represents a single point of failure. Without documented redundancy, failover, or clustering, this target is at high risk.

risks.description: RPC Broker server failure causes complete system unavailability with no documented failover mechanism
risks.rationale: Single RPC Broker instance is a single point of failure; any server failure exceeds 3-minute downtime allowance for 99.5% availability

risks.description: Database layer (FileMan/M Software) failure causes extended outage without documented high-availability configuration
risks.rationale: No documented database clustering or replication; database failure recovery time likely exceeds 3-minute threshold

risks.description: Network infrastructure failure between client and RPC Broker causes unavailability
risks.rationale: No documented network redundancy or alternative communication paths

sensitivity_points.parameter: RPC Broker server mean time between failures (MTBF)

conflicting_attributes: Availability (positive: asynchronous replication enables faster failover)
Reliability (negative: asynchronous replication risks data loss during failover)

scenario_reference.text: Server-side transaction management ensures 100% data consistency for patient record updates with automatic rollback on failure

analysis_records.stimulus: Patient record update transaction with potential failure conditions
analysis_records.response_measure: 100% data consistency with automatic rollback on failure
architectural_decisions: Server-side transaction management in RPC Broker and database layer
Centralized business logic enforcement
FileMan database with ACID transaction support
analysis_records.reasoning: Server-side transaction management centralizes consistency enforcement. The RPC Broker and FileMan database provide transaction boundaries. However, distributed transaction coordination between RPC Broker and database requires careful implementation.

risks.description: Partial transaction commit if RPC Broker fails after database commit but before client acknowledgment
risks.rationale: Two-phase commit coordination between RPC Broker and database may fail in edge cases, leaving inconsistent state

non_risks.description: FileMan database ACID properties ensure atomic transaction execution within database layer
non_risks.rationale: FileMan provides robust transaction management with automatic rollback on database-level failures

non_risks.description: Centralized server-side logic prevents client-side transaction inconsistencies
non_risks.rationale: All business logic and transaction coordination occurs on server, eliminating client-side consistency risks

conflicting_attributes: Performance (negative: higher isolation reduces concurrency and increases latency)

scenario_reference.text: The layered architecture supports incremental migration from MUMPS to web-based services while maintaining operational continuity

analysis_records.stimulus: Requirement to migrate from MUMPS to web-based services incrementally
architectural_decisions: Layered architecture with clear separation between database, middleware, and client layers
RPC Broker abstraction layer
Web services as alternative access path
analysis_records.reasoning: The layered architecture and RPC Broker abstraction enable incremental migration by isolating changes to specific layers. Web services provide a modern access path while maintaining legacy RPC Broker support. However, dual-path maintenance increases complexity.

risks.description: Maintaining functional parity between legacy RPC Broker and new web services during migration increases complexity and error risk
risks.rationale: Dual access paths require synchronized feature development and testing; inconsistencies could disrupt operations

risks.description: Database layer migration from MUMPS may require schema changes that break RPC Broker compatibility
risks.rationale: FileMan/M Software schema is tightly coupled to MUMPS; migration to modern database may require RPC Broker refactoring

non_risks.description: Layered architecture successfully isolates client layer from database layer changes
non_risks.rationale: Clear layer boundaries allow database migration without requiring client application updates

non_risks.description: Web services provide platform-independent access, enabling gradual client migration
non_risks.rationale: Standard web service protocols allow new clients to access system while legacy clients continue using RPC Broker

sensitivity_points.parameter: API compatibility layer completeness between RPC Broker and web services

conflicting_attributes: Modifiability (positive: incremental migration reduces risk and maintains continuity)
Conceptual Integrity (negative: dual-path maintenance during migration creates architectural inconsistency)
Modifiability (positive: thick abstraction layer isolates changes better)
Performance (negative: additional abstraction layers add latency)

scenario_reference.text: When the RPC Broker server experiences failure, the system provides read-only access to cached patient records within 30 seconds

analysis_records.response_measure: Read-only access to cached patient records within 30 seconds
architectural_decisions: Client-side caching of patient records
Graceful degradation to read-only mode
Failure detection and mode switching mechanism
analysis_records.reasoning: Client-side caching enables continued read access during server failure. However, cache consistency, staleness, and the 30-second detection/switching time are critical parameters not fully specified in the architecture.

risks.description: Client cache may contain stale patient data if cache invalidation mechanism fails
risks.rationale: No documented cache coherence protocol; clients may display outdated medical information during degraded mode

risks.description: 30-second failover detection time may be insufficient if failure detection relies on timeout-based mechanisms
risks.rationale: Network timeout-based detection typically requires 60+ seconds; achieving 30-second detection requires active health monitoring

risks.description: Inconsistent client behavior if some clients detect failure faster than others
risks.rationale: No documented centralized failure notification; clients may operate in mixed modes causing user confusion

non_risks.description: Read-only access to cached data is architecturally feasible with client-side caching
non_risks.rationale: Client applications can maintain local cache and switch to read-only mode without server interaction

sensitivity_points.parameter: Client cache size and retention policy

conflicting_attributes: Availability (positive: frequent updates reduce cache staleness during degraded mode)
Performance (negative: frequent cache updates increase network traffic and server load)
Availability (positive: aggressive detection enables faster failover)
Reliability (negative: aggressive detection may cause false positives and unnecessary mode switches)
